summary: Gruber responds to Dori Smith's argument that web conference organizers show gender bias by failing to invite women JavaScript experts. Smith pointed to 10 women among Amazon's bestselling JavaScript book authors as evidence of an available talent pool being ignored. Gruber does the math more rigorously, finding that women constitute 8.5% of the 106 unique authors on Amazon's JavaScript bestseller list. He then notes that women made up 16.5% of speakers at recent web conferences — nearly double the rate found among JavaScript book authors. He concludes that the numbers don't support the claim of gender bias by conference organizers.
key_points: Dori Smith identified 10 women among Amazon's top 100 JavaScript books as evidence conference organizers were ignoring qualified women
Gruber's more thorough analysis found 9 women out of 106 unique authors, or 8.5% of bestselling JavaScript book authors
Women comprised 16.5% of speakers at recent web conferences (excluding BlogHer and SXSW), nearly double the book author rate
The data, when examined proportionally rather than in absolute numbers, undermines rather than supports the claim of conference organizer bias
money_quotes: After removing non-persons (e.g., five of the books were authored by Adobe Systems Inc.) and duplicates (Danny Goodman was listed six times), we're left with 106 unique authors.
I count nine women (the same nine listed by Dori Smith), which works out to 8.5 percent.
There were 73 women out of a total of 441 speakers, or 16.5 percent — which is almost double the rate of women authors in Amazon's list of bestselling JavaScript books.
I'm not saying these numbers prove anything, but I am saying they certainly do not prove any sort of gender bias on the part of the conference organizers.
key_insight: When you actually do the proportional math on Smith's own chosen metric, women are represented at web conferences at nearly double the rate they appear among bestselling JavaScript book authors, undermining rather than supporting the claim of organizer bias.
